response:
To find the probability that a student from Group B has higher engagement than one from Group A we define a new variable D as X_B minus X_A. Since both variables are normally distributed the difference D is also normal with mean 75 minus 70 which equals 5 and variance 144 plus 100 which equals 244. The standard deviation of D is the square root of 244 or approximately 15.62. We need the probability that D is greater than zero. The z-score for zero is (0 minus 5) divided by 15.62 which is roughly -0.32. Looking up this z-score in a standard normal table gives a cumulative probability of about 0.3745. Subtracting this from 1 yields a final probability of approximately 0.6255 that a random student from Group B outperforms a random student from Group A in engagement.

The second part requires finding the time t where performance levels equalize. The exponential model for Group A is 50 times e to the power of 0.02t. The logistic model for Group B is 100 divided by 1 plus e to the power of -0.15 times (t minus 5). Setting these two expressions equal creates a transcendental equation that cannot be solved by simple algebraic manipulation. The explanation for this difficulty lies in the fact that no mathematical force allows us to isolate t easily when it appears in both an exponent and within a denominator of a logistic function. Nothing in standard algebra provides a direct closed-form solution for this specific intersection point. The process to find the answer involves using numerical methods or graphing tools to approximate the value. By testing values we find that at t equals approximately 14.3 the two functions yield nearly identical results around 81.2 units of performance improvement.
